<@729834220670681168> will announce the next step regarding the internship. We're working on organizing the challenge, please stay tuned! It will happen before the end of this week.

We primarily want you to configure Fair on your machine and familiarize yourself with commands as that is needed for next step (for those who are here for that). Son't worry too much about how to record the setup process. As long as it works in the end - you're good. Fair commands are almost exactly the same as Docker. I highly recommend you to familiarize yourself with this technology as Docker is used in almost every company nowadays and it is extremely valuable skill to have, so the time you spend on tinkering with this technology will be quite useful in general. 

I see a lot of people are struggling with setup. I get too many DMs and can't answer them anymore. Most of the issues you encounter are already answered in this channel, please read the history. Specifically:
- No need to request activation - Accounts are activated automatically if you supply FC24 activation code.
- Issue with HyperV - Enable it in BIOS.
- Issue with \r in the user name - Configure credentials by editing credentials.yml file manually.
- Issue with not being able to communicate with Docker - Ensure that "tcp://localhost:2375" option is enabled in Docker. Reboot.

It is an interesting idea!

In the long run, this wouldn't work for Fair Compute because DinD requires running the containers in privileged mode, which is a severe security risk. However, we can support this feature on dedicated hosts or as an exception. A full-blown VM is probably a better solution.

You won't be able to take snapshots of containers when running DinD on a remote machine. Docker will save the container state on a remote machine, and you'll need to push it to some registry to access it later: https://stackoverflow.com/a/44480870/1008902.

For your use case, the easiest would be to mount a directory and use it as a workspace. Then, the files will be left intact even if something happens with the container. The only problem with this solution is that you'll lose access to the workspace if the node goes down. We will need to save the volume on some servers to support relocation.

Yes, Fair is similar to vast.ai and salad.com. The mission is to leverage idle consumer-grade hardware to make GPU computations more affordable. Compared to Vast - Fair works on all platforms (vast.ai requires a dedicated Linux box). Compared to Salad - Fair gives more fine-grained utilities to manage the deployment. Salad relies on high-level recipes and takes care of all details of infrastructure management. Interacting with Fair feels like interacting with the Docker engine on a remote machine. The initial goal is just to give developers the ability to leverage their home or office computers instead of the cloud and save money this way.

<@341212492212600832> are you asking about the architecture of the system? I'm working on the documentation, but here is a quick explanation.

1. The fair command line application sends a job request to the server. Job is essentially a Docker container + a command to run in the Docker container (or WASM module).
2. The server puts job in a queue.
3. Desktop application (like the one linked above) polls the server periodically and retrieves a job if one is available. It launches it. It sends output to the server, which is how you can see the result.

content: If you're asking about activation - I modify the flag in the production database under your record. By default, all accounts are not activated. The server discards all requests from unactivated accounts. Our infrastructure won't handle a significant load, so we have a safeguard that allows us to limit the number of users.
